title: This Can’t Stay Internal
pressure: A patient files a formal complaint. They explicitly point out that two doctors recommended completely different treatments for the same condition. The patient questions the integrity of the entire office. This is no longer internal inconsistency — this is external credibility damage. The case may escalate beyond the office if not handled correctly.
tradeoff: Protect the office vs face the truth of the system

decision_context.what_changed: The issue is now visible to the outside

choices.label: Acknowledge inconsistency as a system issue
Focus on resolving the patient only

A.escalation_text: Calling it a misunderstanding reduces immediate pressure. But the inconsistency remains unaddressed. If another case surfaces, the explanation becomes less credible.
identity_clash: We need to protect the office
If we deny it, we are reinforcing it

second_choices.label: Maintain the narrative
second_choices.cost: Short-term credibility holds while inconsistent practice stays invisible until the next complaint surfaces.

second_choices.label: Re-examine internal consistency
second_choices.cost: You invite internal scrutiny that may surface uncomfortable truths before the organization feels ready.

action_decision.prompt: If this repeats, what breaks first?

choices.label: Audit similar treatment decisions across providers

B.escalation_text: Acknowledging the issue increases short-term risk. It may expose internal inconsistency. But it is the only path that allows structural correction.
identity_clash: But this is the real issue
If we don’t face it now, it will escalate again

second_choices.label: Keep acknowledgment internal
second_choices.cost: You absorb reputational risk privately while patients and staff still see mixed external signals.

second_choices.label: Define and escalate system inconsistency
second_choices.cost: You widen visibility of dysfunction and may accelerate scrutiny before mitigation plans exist.

action_decision.prompt: If this is systemic, what must change?

choices.label: Map treatment decision inconsistencies across providers

C.escalation_text: The patient may be satisfied, but the system remains unchanged. The same issue is likely to reappear with another patient.

second_choices.label: Prioritize resolution speed
second_choices.cost: The patient quiets down while the system that produced divergent treatment plans stays unchanged.

second_choices.label: Investigate root cause
second_choices.cost: You slow closure under urgency and must justify why depth matters before people want a simple ending.

action_decision.prompt: Is resolution the same as correction?

choices.label: Analyze decision patterns behind the case

D.escalation_text: Assigning blame creates immediate clarity. But it simplifies a systemic issue into an individual error.
identity_clash: But this pattern existed before this case
Blame may hide the real issue

second_choices.label: Hold individual responsible
second_choices.cost: Closure is fast; systemic contributors stay unnamed and the pattern can repeat with a new face.

second_choices.label: Separate system vs individual factors
second_choices.cost: Shared analysis takes time and can frustrate stakeholders who want a single named culprit now.
